questions.text: A landlord collects a deposit of £1,800 from a new tenant whose monthly rent is £1,500 (annual rent £18,000). Which statement BEST describes the landlord's position?
opts: The deposit is within the permitted limit as it is less than two months' rent
The deposit exceeds the permitted cap under the Tenant Fees Act 2019 and must be reduced
The deposit is acceptable as there is no cap for monthly rents above £1,000
The deposit cap only applies at the start of the tenancy
optExpl: Incorrect — the cap is five or six weeks, not two months
Correct — annual rent £18,000 < £50,000, so cap is 5 weeks = £1,730.77; £1,800 exceeds this
questions.expl: Annual rent £18,000 < £50,000 threshold → maximum deposit = five weeks' rent: £1,500×12÷52×5 = £1,730.77. The landlord has charged £1,800, exceeding the cap by £69.23.
questions.key: 🔑 Annual rent < £50,000 → 5 weeks max | ≥ £50,000 → 6 weeks max

questions.text: Indicate whether each circumstance would or would not prevent a landlord from serving a valid Section 21 notice.

rows.left: Deposit protected, but prescribed information not served on the tenant
Landlord has a valid EPC exemption registered on the PRS Exemptions Register
Tenant complained to the local authority about disrepair four months ago
Tenancy is currently within the fixed term — three months remaining
Landlord did not carry out a right to rent check before the tenancy began
Current Gas Safety Certificate expired two weeks ago

questions.expl:
• Prescribed information not served → prevents S21 even if deposit is protected
• Registered EPC exemption → does not prevent (compliant state)
• Complaint 4 months ago → prevents (retaliatory eviction, 6-month rule, Deregulation Act 2015)
• Fixed term still running → S21 cannot be served
• Right to rent not done → does NOT prevent S21 (separate obligation, not S21 precondition)
• Gas Safety Certificate expired → prevents S21
questions.key: ⚠️ Trap: Right to rent check failure does NOT invalidate Section 21
questions.ref: Housing Act 1988 s.21; Deregulation Act 2015 ss.33–41

questions.text: A tenant reports the boiler has stopped working in January. Under Section 11 of the Landlord and Tenant Act 1985, which obligations does the landlord have? (Select all that apply.)
opts: Repair the boiler within a reasonable timeframe after being notified
Provide the tenant with alternative heating while repair is arranged
Keep the installations for the supply of gas in repair and proper working order
Replace the boiler with a more energy-efficient model if it cannot be repaired
Reimburse the tenant for costs incurred due to lack of heating
optExpl: Correct — Section 11 requires repair within reasonable time after notification
Incorrect — providing temporary heating is good practice but not a s.11 statutory obligation
Correct — s.11 expressly includes gas supply installations
Incorrect — obligation is to repair, not upgrade
Incorrect — compensation for consequential loss is tort law, not s.11 directly
questions.expl: Section 11 LTA 1985 covers: (1) structure and exterior; (2) water, gas, electricity and sanitation installations; (3) space heating and hot water. Temporary heating (B) and upgrading (D) are not s.11 requirements.
questions.key: 🔑 S11 = Structure + Water/Gas/Elec + Heating/Hot water. Nothing more.

questions.text: Fill in the blanks to complete the statements about mandatory HMO licensing.

blanks.sentence: An HMO requires a mandatory licence when occupied by ___ or more persons forming ___ or more separate households.
A landlord without a licence may face a civil penalty of up to ___. Tenants may apply to the FTT for a Rent Repayment Order of up to ___ months' rent.

questions.expl: Mandatory HMO licensing threshold: 5+ persons from 2+ separate households. Three-storey rule abolished in 2018. Civil penalty: up to £30,000. Rent Repayment Order: up to 12 months' rent.

questions.text: A landlord lets a property to a friend for six months at a reduced rent. The friend already owns their own home and will continue living there at weekends. Which tenancy type is MOST appropriate?
opts: An assured shorthold tenancy, as it is for a fixed term of six months
A contractual tenancy, as the property will not be the friend's only or principal home
A licence to occupy, as the arrangement is informal and between friends
A statutory periodic tenancy, as no fixed term has been formally agreed
optExpl: Incorrect — AST requires the property to be the tenant's only or principal home
Correct — not the principal home → Housing Act 1988 does not apply → contractual tenancy
Incorrect — the friend will have exclusive possession, making this a tenancy not a licence
Incorrect — statutory periodic arises when fixed term expires; cannot be created from scratch
questions.expl: An AST requires the dwelling to be the tenant's only or principal home (HA 1988, s.1). The friend continues to own and occupy their own home, so the condition is not met. Use a contractual tenancy.
questions.key: AST prerequisite: only or principal home. Not principal home = no AST.
